mnemonics.title: WT vs WB Memory
mnemonics.content: WT = 'Write Together' (cache + memory together), WB = 'Write Later' (memory updated later when 'Back' aka evicted)

mnemonics.title: Dirty Bit Meaning
mnemonics.content: DIRTY = 'Data In cache That hoRders modIfication since Original puT Yellow' - modified data in cache

mnemonics.title: Write-Through Simplicity
mnemonics.content: W-T = 'Write True' - always writes true data to memory, always accurate/true

acronyms.usage: Cache policy where data written to both cache and main memory simultaneously
Cache policy where data written to cache first, main memory updated later on eviction
Policy on write miss: load block into cache then update
Policy on write miss: write directly to memory, skip cache loading
Write misses bypass cache to prevent pollution

visualTips.visualization: Picture two parallel pipes (cache and memory) - water (data) flows through BOTH pipes on every write, like a Y-split
Picture a buffer tank (cache) that collects water, with a drain pipe (write-back) that empties only when tank is full (eviction)
A light bulb icon: OFF = clean (matches memory), ON (glowing/yellow) = dirty (modified), needs attention before removal
Fork in road: left path = write-allocate (goes to cache first), right path = no-write-allocate (goes straight to memory)

analogies.analogy: Writing in pen on a document AND carbon copy - both always have the same information instantly
Working on a whiteboard with eraser: changes stay on board, you copy to paper (main memory) only when you need to erase
A 'modified' sticker on a file folder - tells you the folder has unsaved changes before you throw it away
A delivery truck loading dock: packages (writes) drop here, truck delivers to main memory later while you continue working
